title: The Tide Pool Records Department
description: You crest a dune and find a row of tide pools, each labeled with a barnacle-crusted brass plaque: RECEIPTS, CORRESPONDENCE, PENDING. Laminated documents drift in the brine while a small crab in a paper visor stamps each page as it floats past, never once looking up. A notice board reads: 'RETRIEVALS BY APPOINTMENT ONLY. APPOINTMENTS AVAILABLE IN POOL FOUR.' Pool Four is much, much deeper than the others.

choices.label: Skim the PENDING pool for anything useful
choices.description: Documents in PENDING are technically still in processing, which means they technically belong to no one. Probably.

choices.label: Extract the gold-stamped folder without making a ripple
choices.description: A folder near the crab gleams with official importance. One ripple and the visor comes up.

choices.label: Quietly swap the RECEIPTS and CORRESPONDENCE plaques
choices.description: Weeks of filing, undone in one polite act of chaos. The crab will figure it out eventually. Probably never.
choices.visibleHint: The crab's stamping rhythm falters, just slightly.

choices.label: Leave a written retrieval request and step back
choices.description: Proper channels. Slow, dignified, and unlikely to end with a crab in your hair.

memorySuggestions.content: A stretch of tide pools operates as a records department run by a crab clerk; retrievals require an appointment booked in the ominously deep Pool Four.

npcSuggestions.name: Clerk Pinch
npcSuggestions.description: A small crab in a paper visor who stamps drifting documents with total dedication and zero eye contact. Deeply protective of the filing system.

title: The Machine That Only Says YES
description: Half-buried in a drift of junk sits a vending machine with no brand, no prices, and a single button labeled YES. Behind the glass, the spiral racks hold a thermos, a coil of rope, a parcel stamped FRAGILE-ISH, and what is unmistakably a sleeping hermit crab. The machine hums a low note that suggests it has been waiting for you, specifically, for some time. A handwritten maintenance log dangles from the coin slot on a piece of string.

choices.label: Read the maintenance log before touching anything
choices.description: Forty-one entries, each in different handwriting, each ending mid-sentence. Knowledge is the safest salvage.

choices.label: Press the YES button
choices.description: It is the only button. It was always going to be pressed. The question is by whom, and you would prefer it be you.
choices.visibleHint: The hum rises half a step as your finger approaches.

choices.label: Reach up through the dispenser flap
choices.description: Skip the negotiations and go straight for the rope coil. Slim arms and steady nerves required.

choices.label: Hum the machine's note back at it
choices.description: Two can hum. Perhaps it just wants harmony. Perhaps it wants a duet partner. Perhaps it dispenses for friends.

npcSuggestions.name: The YES Machine
npcSuggestions.description: A humming vending machine with one button, stocked with a thermos, rope, a FRAGILE-ISH parcel, and a sleeping hermit crab. Its maintenance log has 41 unfinished entries.

title: The Heap of Formerly Important Things
description: You find a junk pile organized with alarming care: every bent oar, dented kettle, and one-eyed doll wears a little tag explaining what it used to mean to somebody. A laminated sign declares: 'TAKE ONLY WHAT TAKES YOU BACK. ONE (1) ITEM PER VISITOR. THE HEAP IS WATCHING.' Nothing visibly watches you, which is somehow worse. Near the summit, something glints in a way that tagged items are not supposed to glint.

choices.label: Scale the heap toward the glint
choices.description: The slope is loose kettles and looser dolls all the way up. Whatever glints up there is untagged, unclaimed, and unstable.

choices.label: Read tags until one reads you back
choices.description: Somewhere in here is an item whose tag describes something you lost. The heap seems to be counting on it.

choices.label: Take exactly one item, exactly as the sign instructs
choices.description: Pick something modest, nod respectfully at the pile, and leave with your conscience and your ankles intact.

choices.label: Pocket a second item while the heap isn't watching
choices.description: Rules are for people who believe junk piles have eyes. The kettle would fit so nicely next to the doll.
choices.visibleHint: The sign did specifically mention that the heap is watching.

memorySuggestions.content: A meticulously catalogued junk heap allows one item per visitor and claims to be watching; every item is tagged with what it used to mean to someone.

title: Now Serving Number Three
description: The shipwreck on the rocks has been refurbished — not repaired, refurbished. Someone bolted a ticket dispenser to the hull, arranged the salvage into a tidy roped queue, and hung a sign reading 'NOW SERVING: 3. YOUR SALVAGE EXPERIENCE MATTERS TO US.' The dispenser offers you ticket number 41. There is no staff, no other customers, and no evidence there ever were any.
goHomeLabel: Mutter that your ticket is for a different wreck

choices.label: Take ticket 41 and sit in the good chair
choices.description: The system exists for a reason. Possibly a terrible reason, but a reason. Wait your turn and see what service arrives.

choices.label: Duck under the velvet rope and start salvaging
choices.description: The queue is a suggestion. The crowbar-sized gap in the hull is an invitation. Be quick about it.

choices.label: Ask the dispenser who exactly is being served right now
choices.description: Number 3 is being served by someone, somewhere. The dispenser has a speaker grille, and you have questions.

memorySuggestions.content: A refurbished shipwreck runs a ticket-queue salvage service permanently stuck at NOW SERVING: 3; who or what actually serves customers remains unknown.

title: Three Out of Ten for Form
description: A row of driftwood planks has washed up arranged like a panel of judges, and as you reach for a promising crate beneath them, the longest plank clears its throat. 'Sloppy approach,' it announces. 'Wide stance, no follow-through. Three out of ten.' The other planks creak in agreement. The crate is right there, but apparently your technique is being scored.

choices.label: Extract the crate with flawless, judge-worthy technique
choices.description: Square the shoulders, soft knees, clean lift. If you must be scored, score a ten.

choices.label: Sincerely ask the panel for professional feedback
choices.description: Everyone started somewhere. The driftwood clearly has opinions, and flattered judges are generous judges.

choices.label: Heckle the panel about their own washed-up careers
choices.description: They are literally washed up. The joke writes itself. Whether the joke ends well is another matter.

choices.label: Attempt the legendary triple-crate lift nobody asked for
choices.description: There is one crate. You will find two more. The panel will remember this forever, one way or the other.

npcSuggestions.name: The Driftwood Panel
npcSuggestions.description: A washed-up row of driftwood planks that scores scavengers' form out loud, holds lifelong grudges, and plays favorites shamelessly.

title: The Lost and Found That Found You
description: A lost-and-found kiosk stands alone in the scrub, its counter bell ringing as you approach even though nobody touches it. On the counter sits a bin labeled DEFINITELY YOURS, full of objects you have never seen before in your life. The claim form has a single question: 'DESCRIBE THE ITEM YOU LOST (BE HONEST, WE CHECK).' You did not lose anything. The bin strongly disagrees.
goHomeLabel: Declare yourself a lost item and exit the premises

choices.label: Rummage through the DEFINITELY YOURS bin
choices.description: Maybe one of these strangers' trinkets really is yours. Maybe ownership works differently out here.

choices.label: Write 'nothing' on the form and submit it truthfully
choices.description: Honesty is its own reward, and occasionally also a processing fee waiver. Let the kiosk sort out the rest.

choices.label: Invent a sentimental umbrella and describe it convincingly
choices.description: Polka dots. A bite mark on the handle from a misunderstanding. If they check, your story had better hold.

memorySuggestions.content: A self-ringing lost-and-found kiosk insists the player lost something; its DEFINITELY YOURS bin holds unfamiliar objects and the claim form warns 'we check.'
